data.title: $90K Bull market support retest? 5 Things to know in Bitcoin this week
data.description:
Bitcoin heads into the end of February in an uncertain mood — can bulls avoid a fresh $90,000 dip?

  • Liquidity is piling up on either side of the spot price as Bitcoin limps around an increasingly tight trading range.

  • US inflation data is due, including the Fed’s “preferred” index, as stagflation woes deepen.

  • Gold looks set for yet another all-time high, while US dollar strength seeks a turnaround from weeks of downside.

  • Bitcoin implied volatility is sinking to levels rarely seen in its history.

  • Market sentiment is bad, and as network activity drops, analysis warns of trouble brewing.

Traders map out BTC price support toward $90,000

Bitcoin remains stuck in a narrow range, data from Cointelegraph Markets Pro and TradingView shows, after the Bybit hack crushed last week’s attempted upside breakout.

The potential for a fresh push by both bulls and bears, however, is there — as depicted by current liquidity conditions on exchange order books.

“Now we can see in the liquidation levels that the liquidations are equal to the downside or upside,” popular trader CrypNuevo wrote in a thread on X on Feb. 23 while discussing the outlook for the week. 

“Perhaps more to the upside considering price is in a LTF downtrend. $94.7k & $92.5k are key.”

Fellow trader Roman was less optimistic, eyeing a return to the bottom of the multimonth trading range.

“So many failed attempts to move up with a major lack of strength,” he told X followers.

“Just seems like 90k support touch is coming. This invalidates if we break 98.4 with a close above. Range is very tight so I’m expecting a quick move.”

On weekly timeframes, meanwhile, trader Luca eyed an incoming test of Bitcoin’s bull market support band.

This area, formed by two moving averages, had functioned as support since early October when broke through previous all-time highs at $73,800.

Luca suggested that Bitcoin was primed for a return to the upside amid low funding rates, poor sentiment and retail investors reducing exposure.

PCE arrives as “stagflation” concerns fester

Markets are waiting for the “final piece to the puzzle” this week as US inflation data continues to offer risk-asset headwinds.

The Personal Consumption Expenditures (PCE) Index, known to be the Federal Reserve’s “preferred” inflation gauge, is due out on Feb. 28.

It will follow initial jobless claims, which last week beat expectations — demonstrating weakening labor market conditions against a backdrop of resurgent inflation markers. This, as Cointelegraph previously reported, is indicative of “stagflation” — and traders are keeping a close eye on its development.

“The potential for a ‘stagflation’ economic environment is a major concern for investors, where slow economic growth is coupled with high levels of inflation,” trading firm Mosaic Asset wrote in the latest edition of its regular newsletter, “The Market Mosaic,” on Feb. 23. 

“But interestingly, the historical data shows that stagflation isn’t necessarily associated with poor stock market performance.” 

Mosaic noted that out of 12 stagflation years since 1930, the S&P 500 mostly finished higher despite economic pressures.

“Since 1930, there have been 12 years that featured a slowing economy but rising inflation. The stock market’s real return was positive in 75% of those instances, with an average annual real return of 16.4% in the S&P 500,” it reported.

The latest estimates from CME Group’s FedWatch Tool nonetheless underscore markets’ lack of faith in economic policy easing.

Lowering of interest rates, for example, is unlikely before July, despite there being two Fed meetings in the interim.

“PCE inflation will be the final piece to the puzzle as both PPI and CPI inflation are rebounding,” trading resource The Kobeissi Letter meanwhile determined about the upcoming data release, predicting an “eventful” final week of the month.

Gold keeps pushing higher

In contrast to Bitcoin and altcoins, one asset is refusing to take any notice of fluctuating economic uncertainty: gold.

The precious metal continues to set new all-time highs and, as of Feb. 24, is working on its highest-ever daily close.

The US dollar index (DXY), which measures dollar strength against a basket of US trading partner currencies, is meanwhile seeking a reversal from a downtrend in place since early December.

While a strong dollar typically pressures risk assets across the board, Kobeissi notes that the current landscape stands out on longer timeframes.

“Since late-July, gold prices are up ~24% while the US Dollar is up ~2% and the 10-year note yield is up ~8%,” it noted in a dedicated X thread on the topic last week. 

“While gold and rates/USD typically have an inverse correlation, they are rising TOGETHER.”

Kobeissi described surging gold demand across the world, calling it the “global safe haven asset” amid worries over US trade policy and tariffs.

“What's even more impressive is that gold is rising WITH the S&P 500,” it continued. 

“In fact, gold has more than DOUBLED the S&P 500's YTD return. In 2024, gold and the S&P 500 had an unprecedented correlation of ~0.81.”

As Cointelegraph reported, Bitcoin has often been seen to copy gold bull run moves with a delay of around three months.

“Gold is ripping, Bitcoin chopping. Summer 2024 vibes,” Charles Edwards, founder of quantitative Bitcoin and digital asset fund Capriole Investments, summarized to X followers earlier this month. 

“You know the deal, Bitcoin will bore you to death. As long as Gold keeps trending, Bitcoin almost always sees a comparable (bigger) breakout within 3-6 months.”

Bitcoin volatility metric challenges records

Bitcoin’s stubborn trading range has led to some rare readings from volatility metrics.

On weekly timeframes, realized volatility, which measures the standard deviation of market returns from its mean, is near record lows.

The trend was noticed by onchain analytics firm Glassnode at the weekend.

“Bitcoin’s 1-week realized volatility has collapsed to 23.42%, nearing historical lows. In the past four years, it has dipped lower only a few times - e.g., Oct 2024 (22.88%) & Nov 2023 (21.35%),” it revealed in an X thread. 

“Similar compressions in the past led to major market moves.”

Glassnode drew similar conclusions from 1-week options realized volatility, now approaching multi-year lows.

“The last time IV was this low (2023, early 2024), major volatility spikes followed. Meanwhile, longer-term IV remains higher (3m: 53.1%, 6m: 56.25%),” it reported.

Low volatility has long been on the radar among Bitcoin traders thanks to the inability of either buyers or sellers to effect a lasting trend change.

Network activity sees a slow bleed

Bitcoin network activity and sentiment are both declining — and analysis warns that this could have long-term implications for price action.

Active wallet addresses continue to shrink in the aftermath of the US Presidential Election, leading onchain analytics platform CryptoQuant to draw comparisons to BTC price dips earlier in the current bull run.

“Additionally, the accumulation rate of Bitcoin spot ETFs has slowed, with recent minor capital outflows observed,” contributor Avocado_onchain wrote in one of its “Quicktake” blog posts on Feb. 23.

“The number of UTXOs is also decreasing, with the magnitude of the decline similar to the correction period in September 2023. If this trend continues, we could see signs of investor exodus similar to the market cycle peak of 2017.”

While the post acknowledged that “a simple decline in UTXOs alone is not enough to confirm the end of the current cycle,” flagging sentiment remains a problem.

The Crypto Fear & Greed Index currently measures 49/100, circling “neutral” territory after remaining flat throughout most of February.

“Since previous bullish narratives have already been priced in, further upside momentum will require either the resolution of uncertainties or new bullish catalysts,” Avocado_onchain summarized.

This article does not contain investment advice or recommendations. Every investment and trading move involves risk, and readers should conduct their own research when making a decision.

data.title: Corporate bosses urge Berlin to quickly move on weak economy
data.description:
German business leaders on Monday called on Berlin to quickly form a new government, warning Europe's top economy could not afford to waste any time as companies suffer from high costs, red tape and rising competition from abroad.

The country's conservative CDU/CSU bloc won Sunday's national election to pave the way for a coalition with the Social Democrats, easing concerns over a more fractious three-way government and prompting the corporate call for the parties to move swiftly.

Germany's top companies have long criticised the government's lack of action over energy costs, among the highest in Europe and more than twice as high as in China and the United States, as well as bureaucracy.

"Germany now needs a government that is able and willing to act - and quickly," said Christian Sewing, CEO of Germany's largest lender Deutsche Bank and president of the association of German banks.

"The challenges facing our country are enormous: The economy urgently needs a fresh start with fundamental reforms."

A recent survey by Germany's Chamber of Commerce and Industry showed 60% of firms citing economic conditions, including bureaucracy and high labour and energy costs, as the biggest business risks, the highest level ever.

This has fuelled fears of deindustrialisation as companies opt to invest outside Germany where conditions are more favourable.

Christian Bruch, CEO of power equipment supplier Siemens Energy , said Germany needed to quickly regain its competitiveness and the country's democratic centrist parties would need to form a coalition as fast as possible.

"Actions in energy policy are crucial for this. The expansion of gas-fired power plants, the strengthening of wind energy, and the modernisation of electricity grids are essential, as is a secure supply of raw materials."

data.title: Global Forex and Fixed Income Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering FX and Fixed Income. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

0841 GMT - The euro is unlikely to rise much further following Sunday's German election, ING analyst Francesco Pesole says in a note. The euro could continue to react as coalition talks start, especially regarding the debt rule, he says. However, German politics will likely soon return to being a small secondary factor for the euro, with the currency driven primarily by U.S. tariff news and U.S.-Russia-Ukraine peace talks. The looming risk of U.S. tariffs and the European Central Bank's loose policy stance should limit euro gains. "Ultimately, we expect to see [the euro] return below $1.04 over the next four weeks." The euro is last up 0.2% at $1.0476. It hit a four-week high of $1.0531 overnight, according to FactSet. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0839 GMT - Europe should forge its own policy on China without relying on direction from the U.S., Spain's chief diplomat says. "Europe must take its own decisions, on its own. And we have to decide when China can be a partner and when China is a competitor," Foreign Minister Jose Manuel Albares tells the Financial Times in an interview. His comments come amid a major shift in geopolitical and trade relations between the U.S. and the rest of the world under President Trump. A good relationship between Europe and China is vital given the country's size and importance for issues like climate change, Albares says. "We need to have an engagement," he says. (joshua.kirby@wsj.com; @joshualeokirby)

0822 GMT - The euro rises after Sunday's German federal election, although the outcome isn't a big deal for the currency market, Commerzbank currency analyst Ulrich Leuchtmann says in a note. As suggested by polls ahead of the election, a coalition between the CDU/CSU alliance and the Social Democrats looks feasible, he says. "The fact that anything euro positive emerged at all is rather surprising and shows that there was a residual fear in the currency market of a destructive result." The euro is also helped by a weaker dollar, he says. The euro rises 0.2% to $1.0485, having hit a four-week high of $1.0531 overnight, according to FactSet. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0822 GMT - Investor spirits have been lifted on the hope that the election win by Germany's conservatives will pull the country out of an economic stupor and bolster collective defense spending, Susannah Streeter, head of money and markets at Hargreaves Lansdown, writes. Friedrich Merz, the leader of the CDU/CSU party, has pledged to relax fiscal rules, increase defense spending and boost the economy with much needed investment, she writes. With Russia's aggression in Europe set to remain an unrelenting black cloud, political factions may pull together to boost contributions to NATO, she adds. The German DAX trades up 0.3% at 22359 points. (adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

0818 GMT - RBC Capital Markets expects that German Bund yields will rise near term as bonds are impacted by a looser fiscal stance in Germany, which could come about under a new government after Germany's election. "In the near term, we hold a bearish call and have been short Bunds for quite some time," RBC analysts say in a note. Ten-year Bund yields briefly rose above 2.50% last week and RBC expects them to rise back above that level soon. They also see potential for a further rise towards 2.75% going into spring. A more business-friendly government and a looser fiscal stance should lift economic expectations, which is negative for bond markets, they say. The 10-year Bund yield is last up 1 basis point at 2.475%. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0807 GMT - Chinese policymakers are likely to trim their GDP growth target to around 4.5% to 5.0%, down from around 5% last year, due to greater external uncertainty, HSBC Global Research analysts write in a note. External demand may drag on growth this year, with rising geopolitical tensions since Trump's second administration. Beijing may acknowledge these external and domestic challenges and emphasize its commitment to stabilizing growth, they add. The analysts note that the 10% across-the-board tariffs on China, enacted since February 4, align with their base case However, there is a possibility of further tariffs as Trump's "America First" trade executive order requires certain departments to publish trade reports by April 1, potentially leading to additional trade actions against China, they say.(jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

0803 GMT - Dollar purchases ahead of month-end rebalancing of portfolios look weak, Barclays analysts say in a note. The dollar has come off its recent peak as positive sentiment surrounding the currency has retraced, they say. This largely increases asset values in dollars outside the U.S. and raises the need to buy dollars at month-end for rebalancing. The outperformance of European equities compared to the U.S. and appreciation of the Japanese yen against the dollar "contributed to a moderate dollar-buying signal against both currencies at month-end," they say. The DXY dollar index falls 0.2% to 106.409 after hitting a two-and-a-half-month low of 106.126 overnight. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0800 GMT - The new German parliament will find it difficult reform the debt brake and free up spending on defense or infrastructure, Deutsche Bank economists say in a note. Together the CDU/CSU, SPD and Greens hold just under the required two-thirds majority for constitutional changes, meaning any change to the debt brake would rely on the support of the Left or AfD, they say. "This could be seen as a negative by markets in terms of reducing the likelihood of a decisive fiscal regime shift in Germany," the economists say. However, while the Left campaigned for lowering defense spending, they are in favor of higher infrastructure investments, so there could be cross-party consensus on freeing up more funds, they note. (edward.frankl@wsj.com)

0757 GMT - German government bond yields edge higher in early trade as markets welcome the results of Sunday's election. The Christian Democrats made a convincing victory, positioning Friedrich Merz as the likely next Chancellor. The next government is most likely to be a Grand coalition between the Christian Democratic Union and its Bavarian sister party CSU alongside the Social Democrats. The 10-year Bund yield rises 1 basis points to 2.473%, according to Tradeweb. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0752 GMT - The European economy would benefit if the European Central Bank were to cut its key interest rate below the neutral level--estimated by the ECB at around 2%--, says Mediolanum International Funds Ltd's Marco Meijer. In the near term, a more fragmented global economy and rising deficits are unlikely to provide a significant boost to growth, which should put downward pressure on Bund yields, bringing them closer to 2% by the summer, he says. It remains uncertain whether the ECB's key rate will fall below 2%, however. "There is not yet a clear commitment for it to go below that level," the deputy head of fixed income says. The 10-year Bund yield rises 0.5 basis points to 2.468%, according to Tradeweb. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0737 GMT - A fresh start for Germany looks less likely after the center-right won less convincingly than had been expected, Joerg Kraemer at Commerzbank writes in a note to investors. The conservatives under Friedrich Merz came out on top in Sunday's parliamentary elections but Merz faces long and tough negotiations to form a government with other parties. A coalition with the outgoing centre-left party looks likeliest but the two parties would have only a slim majority in the Bundestag. And while the two parties would likely agree on an initial cash boost for infrastructure, their differences outweigh their similarities, Kraemer says. "This dampens the hope that there will be a fresh start in economic policy," he says. (joshua.kirby@wsj.com; @joshualeokirby)

0731 GMT - A decisive fiscal regime shift in Germany seems unlikely after centrist parties failed to win a constitutional majority, analysts at Deutsche Bank write. Even with the support of the green party, the conservative party CDU/CSU and Chancellor Olaf Scholz's Social Democratic Party jointly hold just under 66% of seats, they add. Constitutional changes, including to the country's fiscal rules, known as debt brake, would rely on the support of far-left Die Linke or far-right AfD, the analysts write. This could be seen as negative by investors as its reduces the likelihood of a decisive shift in Germany's fiscal framework, the analysts add. (adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

The euro could continue to react as coalition talks start, especially regarding the debt rule, he says. data.title: Luyin Investment Group Plans to Increase Stake in Wanhua Energy-Saving
data.description:
Luyin Investment Group plans to increase its holdings in China Energy Conservation Wanhua through centralized bidding or block transactions.

The investment will range from 125 million yuan to 250 million yuan, according to a Friday filing with the Shanghai bourse.

Currently, Luyin and its subsidiary hold 10.16% of Wanhua's shares. The move aims to optimize asset allocation and boost investor confidence.

The board has authorized implementation within 12 months, ensuring no disruption to daily operations.

data.title: Thomas Cook (India) Arm Opens New Resort in Jaisalmer, India
data.description:
Sterling Holiday Resorts, a wholly-owned subsidiary of travel services provider Thomas Cook (India) has launched a new resort named Sterling Rudraksh at Jaisalmer in Rajasthan, Indian, according to a Monday filing to the Indian stock exchanges.

With this, Sterling now has 59 resorts in 50 sestinations across India, includign nine resorts in the state of Rajasthan.

Thomas Cook shares were up over 1% in recent trade.

data.title: Nvidia Earnings, Inflation: What to Watch This Week — WSJ
data.description:
By WSJ Staff

Last, but not least. The final member of the so-called Magnificent Seven tech stocks to report in this earnings season is Nvidia, which will update investors late Wednesday.

Those results, from the semiconductor company at the center of the artificial-intelligence boom, are likely to be the highlight of next week's corporate news.

On the economic front, one of the most important data points will be a readout from the Federal Reserve's preferred inflation gauge.

Here's a rundown of what to look out for:

Today

  • Earnings: Domino's Pizza is among companies due to report before the market opens. Results from videoconferencing company Zoom Communications are slated for after the close.

Tomorrow

  • Earnings: Home Depot and Keurig Dr. Pepper are set to report before the open. Workday is among companies due to report after the close.
  • Data: The S&P CoreLogic Case-Shiller home-price indexes are due at 9 a.m. ET. A consumer confidence readout from the Conference Board is due an hour later.

Wednesday

  • Nvidia earnings: The chip maker is due to report full-year earnings after the market closes.
  • Other earnings: Results are due premarket from Lowe's and others. After the close, eBay and Salesforce are due to report.
  • Data: A report on new-home sales is due at 10 a.m.

Thursday

  • Data: Weekly jobless claims, data on durable goods and a revised estimate of last quarter's gross-domestic product are all due at 8:30 a.m. In late January, a first GDP estimate pegged growth at an annualized 2.3% in the fourth quarter.
  • Earnings: Hormel Foods, the company behind Skippy peanut butter and Spam, is due to report before the opening bell, as are J.M. Smucker and Warner Bros. Discovery. Computer companies Dell and HP are due to report after the close.

Friday

  • Inflation: PCE data for January is due at 8:30 a.m. The personal-consumption-expenditures price index is the Fed's favored measure of inflation.

This item is part of a Wall Street Journal live coverage event. The full stream can be found by searching P/WSJL (WSJ Live Coverage).

data.title: Heard on the Street Friday Recap: UnitedHealth Woes — WSJ
data.description:
By Hearn Editors

The Justice Department is investigating UnitedHealth's Medicare billing practices, The Wall Street Journal reported. UnitedHealth's shares fell 7%, weighing on the Dow industrials. Other healthcare stocks, including CVS Health, Elevance and Humana, sold off. Heard on the Street's David Wainer says the industry is at a reckoning point.

Weak economic data and the UnitedHealth news pressured stock indexes. Consumer confidence slid, and existing-home sales declined 4.9% in January. The Dow and the S&P 500 each dropped 1.7%. The Nasdaq Composite fell 2.2%. Recap with this podcast.

Bybit, one of the world's largest crypto exchanges, reported a huge hack. More than $1.4 billion worth of ether was stolen, making it the biggest crypto heist of all time, according to blockchain analytics firm Certik.

Novo Nordisk shares rose 5%. The Food and Drug Administration said there was no longer a shortage of the pharmaceutical company's hugely popular Ozempic and Wegovy obesity drugs. Shares of telehealth company Hims & Hers, which sells compound obesity drugs, dropped 26%.

Bond yields continued their gradual descent. The 10-year Treasury yield declined 0.08 percentage point to 4.419%. Yields have fallen for four consecutive weeks.

Check out the What's News audio summary of the week in markets, and catch up on the biggest stock-market movers of the week in the latest Score column.

This analysis comes from the Journal's Heard on the Street team. Subscribe to their free daily afternoon newsletter here.

This item is part of a Wall Street Journal live coverage event. The full stream can be found by searching P/WSJL (WSJ Live Coverage).

data.title: French Stocks Kick Off the Week in Red
data.description:
The CAC 40 dropped 0.6% to 8,111 on Monday, following a 0.4% gains in the previous session, weighed down by notable losses across several sectors.

Schneider Electric emerged as the biggest drag, plunging 4.8%, while Legrand declined 1.9%, Accor dropped 1.6%, and ArcelorMittal fell 1.3%.

Moreover, luxury stocks were hit hard, with Kering declining 0.9%, LVMH dropping 1%, and Hermes losing 1.3%.

On the other hand, Thales led the index with a 1.5% gain, fueled by hopes of increased defense spending amid growing concerns over a potential reduction in U.S. support for Ukraine.

data.title: Almirall jumps with FY EBITDA growth, outlook
data.description:
** Shares in Almirall rise 7.5%, one of the best performers on the Madrid General Index (.SMSI), after the Spanish drugmaker said its FY EBITDA grew 10.6% year-on-year to 192.6 million euros ($201.84 million)

** Dermatology sales were up 17.8% year-on-year and the company recorded a net profit of 10.1 million euros after a net loss of 38,5 million euros in 2023, but below a consensus of 17.8 million euros gain

** Jefferies says net sales are broadly in-line with cons and its own estimates, adds EBITDA is 3% ahead of consensus but impairments drive a net income miss

** "Cons is in-line with initial 2025 implied sales and EBITDA outlook", Jefferies says

** The broker adds also Almirall is aiming for a high double digit organic CAGR in biologics sales, driven by Ebglyss' strong performance in EU, plus Ilumetri's robust growth in an increasingly competitive market

** Jefferies underlines Ebglyss opportunity still not priced-in and sees a robust 11.5% net sales growth with operating leverage then driving an attractive +34.8% adjusted EPS CAGR over 2024-27E

** Up to the previous session's close, shares were up 10.7%​ YTD

($1 = 0.9542 euros)

data.title: Motorola Solutions Acquires RapidDeploy Emergency Response Platform
data.description:
Motorola Solutions said Sunday it has acquired RapidDeploy, a cloud-based emergency response platform for public safety services located in Austin, Texas.

Financial terms were not disclosed.

Motorola said RapidDeploy's Next Generation 911 product suite speeds up emergency response and improves decision-making by service providers.

The platform includes real-time call location mapping for crisis response, the company said.

data.title: MIS Sells OpenAI Stake for $8 Million
data.description:
Al Moammar Information Systems (SASE:7200), d/b/a MIS, sold its $5 million investment in Microsoft-backed artificial intelligence startup OpenAI for $8.4 million.

The Saudi Arabia-based information technology company said Monday that the move is consistent with the company's strategy to pursue other investments.

In 2024, the company allocated 40 million Saudi riyals from existing funds to invest in global AI companies.

data.title: Bund yields struggle for direction after German elections
data.description:
Euro area benchmark Bund yields struggled for direction on Monday as the outcome of German general elections supported expectations for pro-growth policies, though uncertainty remained over potential reforms to increase fiscal spending.

Germany's likely next chancellor, Friedrich Merz, will start trying to form a coalition government on Monday after his conservative bloc won a national election that saw far-right and far-left parties gain support from disaffected voters.

"We expect a two-party coalition between the (conservative bloc) CDU/CSU and the centre-left SPD to enact some pro-growth supply-side reforms," said Holger Schmieding, chief economist at Berenberg Bank.

He argued that populist parties from the political fringes, such as Alternative for Germany (AfD) and The Left, "can veto any loosening of the debt brake enshrined in the constitution".

Investors have pinned hopes on a new government reforming the so-called debt brake to increase fiscal spending and boost the economy, especially given the pressure from the U.S. government for European countries to pay more for defence.

Germany's 10-year bond yield (DE10YT=RR), which serves as the benchmark for the wider euro zone, was down 0.5 basis points (bps) at 2.46%, after rising around 2 bps in early trade.

The Left has campaigned for lowering defence spending, but they are in favour of higher infrastructure investments and abolishing the debt brake.

"There could, therefore, be a cross-party consensus with the Left on setting up an off-budget infrastructure fund or exempting infrastructure investment from the debt brake to create more room for defence spending in the core budget," creating some "room for manoeuvre," according to Deutsche Bank.

The 2-year yield (DE2YT=RR) dropped one bp to 2.09%.

Merz will probably face lengthy coalition negotiations after the far-right Alternative for Germany (AfD) surged to a historic second place after Chancellor Olaf Scholz's three-way alliance collapsed.

"The risk that coalition building fails because the CDU/CSU pulls out or SPD members vote down the result is significant, in our view," said Christian Schulz, an economist at Citi.

"That could lead to either a minority government or snap elections," he added.

Schulz highlighted the differences between the two parties, saying that the CDU/CSU campaigned to enhance competitiveness by cutting taxes for business, while SPD campaigned for a demand boost, with another minimum wage hike and a 100 billion euros public investment fund.

Italy's 10-year yield was down 0.5 bps at 3.55%. The yield gap between Italian and German yields (DE10IT10=RR) widened to 108.5 bps.

data.title: Market Chatter: Logistics Plot in Tsing Yi, Hong Kong Worth Up to HK$3.7 Billion Gets One Bid
data.description:
A plot for logistics purposes in Tsing Yi, Hong Kon received one bind, The Standard reported Friday, citing the city's Transport and Logistics Bureau.

The site spans 477,000 square feet with a maximum buildable area of about 2.5 million square feet valued between HK$2.45 billion and HK$3.7 billion, the report said

The plot will be used for logistics services development and public parking, the report said.

(Market Chatter news is derived from conversations with market professionals globally. This information is believed to be from reliable sources but may include rumor and speculation. Accuracy is not guaranteed.)

data.title: Prosus 4 bln euro takeover of Just Eat drags its shares, sends food delivery up
data.description:
** Shares in Prosus fall 6% after the Dutch tech investor said it would buy Just Eat Takeaway for 4.1 billion euros ($4.3 billion) to create an European food delivery "champion"

** Shares in Glovo owner Delivery Hero , where Prosus is also the biggest shareholder, are up 5% after gaining as much as 9% following the announcement

** "Some analysts see this as first step for a merger with Delivery Hero," a German trader says

** Shares in Just Eat Takeaway skyrocket 55% to match the premium of the offer

** Shares of London-listed peer Deliveroo rise 6%

** Meanwhile, Naspers (NPNJh.J), majority owner of Prosus, was down 6.33% in Johannesburg at 0744 GMT

($1 = 0.9548 euros)

data.title: Berachain TVL surges above $3.2B, overtaking Base and Arbitrum
data.description:
Layer-1 blockchain Berachain passed a milestone with its total value locked (TVL) surpassing $3.26 billion, making it the sixth-largest blockchain network in decentralized finance (DeFi), according to DeFi data tracker DefiLlama.

As of Feb. 24, Berachain’s TVL exceeded that of Arbitrum and Base, marking a significant achievement for the network. At the time of writing, the Berachain (BERA) token was trading at $6.75, with a market capitalization of $715 million and a fully diluted valuation (FDV) of $3.3 billion.

TVL is the total value of crypto assets locked in a smart contract, a metric that often affects the overall value of DeFi projects. When the TVL of a network increases, it’s usually followed by an expansion of liquidity, popularity and usability. 

A higher TVL means more capital is locked in a network’s DeFi protocols, so participants in its ecosystem may get more yields. Lower TVLs imply lower capital availability, resulting in fewer proceeds for DeFi. 

Berachain becomes the sixth largest in DeFi TVL

With its TVL surging past $3.26 billion, the network has surpassed popular networks, including Arbitrum, which has a TVL of $2.9 billion, and Base, with $3.24 billion. The blockchain now holds 2.98% of the value locked in the entire DeFi space and has the sixth-largest TVL. 

Liquid staking protocol Infrared Finance leads the network with a TVL of $1.52 billion, followed by decentralized exchange (DEX) Kodiak at $1.12 billion and yield farming protocol Concrete, which holds nearly $800 million.

Meanwhile, Ethereum remains the dominant force in DeFi, with a TVL of $58 billion, representing 53.4% of the total DeFi market. Solana ranks second with $8 billion in locked assets, holding a 7.45% market share.

Can Berachain challenge Solana and Ether’s dominance?

Vance Spencer, the co-founder of Framework Ventures, which co-led a $100 million Series B round for Berachain, said in a Cointelegraph interview last September that Berachain’s token could be the next major Ether competitor.

Spencer said that the network’s proof-of-liquidity consensus could usher in a fully-aligned blockchain ecosystem. “When you stake BERA, you have to direct the liquidity you get toward these primitives. And so all the fees stay in the ecosystem,” he said. 

On Feb. 6, the Bera Foundation distributed 80 million BERA tokens to eligible users. The tokens are estimated to be worth $632 million, making the airdrop one of the largest in crypto history.

data.title: Europe Must Decide Its Own China Approach, Spanish FM Says — Market Talk
data.description: Europe should forge its own policy on China without relying on direction from the U.S., Spain's chief diplomat says. "Europe must take its own decisions, on its own. And we have to decide when China can be a partner and when China is a competitor," Foreign Minister Jose Manuel Albares tells the Financial Times in an interview. His comments come amid a major shift in geopolitical and trade relations between the U.S. and the rest of the world under President Trump. A good relationship between Europe and China is vital given the country's size and importance for issues like climate change, Albares says. "We need to have an engagement," he says. (joshua.kirby@wsj.com; @joshualeokirby)

data.title: SES to Partner with Quvia for Enhanced Multi-orbit Connectivity
data.description:
Satellite communications provider SES agreed to partner with Quvia to boost its multi-orbit connectivity through enhanced network orchestration capabilities.

Quvia, which provides an AI-backed platform for ships, planes, and remote sites, will integrate SES's Adaptive Resource Control system with its Grid and Network Capacity Controller, according to a Monday release.

data.title: US 10-Year Treasury Yield at 2-1/2-Month Lows
data.description:
The yield on the US 10-year Treasury note was little changed around 4.4% on Monday, the lowest level since mid-December, as traders weighed President Trump's trade policies, fresh economic data, and the outlook for monetary policy.

This week's key focus will be the PCE report, which could offer further insights into inflation trends, while the second estimate of Q1 2025 GDP growth is expected to confirm a 2.3% annualized expansion.

Last week, flash S&P Global PMI data showed an unexpected contraction in the services sector, while manufacturing growth accelerated.

However, input prices climbed, and business expectations weakened amid rising uncertainty over government policies.

The data heightened concerns about the US economic outlook, leading traders to increase bets on potential Fed rate cuts.

data.title: Piraeus Bank sees stable growth by 2028 after strong 2024 profit
data.description:
Piraeus Bank, Greece's third-largest lender by market value, on Monday reported a double-digit rise in 2024 profit on higher net interest income and increased lending.

Net earnings came in at 1.1 billion euros ($1.15 billion), up from 788 million euros in 2023.

Piraeus said profit remained stable at about the same level each year by 2028.

($1 = 0.9532 euros)

data.title: Japan's Inflation Accelerates to 4% in January as Food Prices Surge
data.description:
Japan's annual inflation rate rose to 4% in January from 3.6% a month earlier, reaching its highest level since January 2023, government data showed on Friday.

The increase was driven by a 7.8% jump in food prices, the steepest gain in 15 months, as fresh vegetables and other fresh food saw sharp cost increases.

Energy costs remained high, with electricity prices up 18% and gas costs rising 6.8%, reflecting the absence of government subsidies since May 2024.

Other price pressures came from transport, which accelerated to 2.0% from 1.1%, and furniture and household items, which saw a 3.4% rise from 3%.

In contrast, communication costs fell 0.3%, while education declined 1.1%.

Core inflation, which excludes volatile fresh food prices, climbed to a 19-month high of 3.2%, exceeding market expectations of 3.1%.

ING Research said fresh food costs are expected to keep rising, while some service prices-especially dining out-could spill over into broader inflation gauges.

The Bank of Japan is also watching whether recent food cost increases, particularly for rice, are being passed on to consumers, ING Research said.

On a monthly basis, consumer prices increased by 0.5%, easing slightly from December's 0.6% gain.

The data keeps inflation above the central bank's 2% target, adding pressure on policymakers as they consider the future of ultra-loose monetary policy.
